item.title: Sad
item.description: They interrogated me. Like 7 cops and detectives like I murdered him. Just as soon as they got there. I almost passed out from shock and they were mostly assholes. And then the one cop that stayed behind to wait for summer funeral to come get him gave me Steve's driver's license back and said, "Don't commit any Medicare fraud." I said, "too soon much?" It was fucked up! I'm sitting here without him. And I'm lost. And I'm pissed off and sad.
item.dc:creator: tokerator

item.dc:creator: deafkitten

item.title: Cake Day? In freaking Pride? Fuck yea!
item.description:
Hello there, my name is Lara and today's my cake day. I don't have any cake to show off, but I've got a bunch of pride pictures I've shot over the years.
It's mostly kisses, flags and happiness in a world that's increasing in hate and bigotry. But we're here still.
You know, I love our resilience as a community. I hate that we have a need to be resilient, but… at least some of us are still here.
This is a month for celebration and remembrance. Celebration for those of us still living the life and remembrance for all those people that we left behind and that we miss so much.
We're queer, we're here, and we've got hip pain — moderately severe.
Let's fly our flags.
Anyway, this is me. A forty-two-year-old woman with a bunch of things to get over. I've got a nice, nice portfolio over at https://prints.larasantaella.es/albums/featured in case any of you want to buy a print or something? Anything would be greatly appreciated because these last three months have been wild.

item.title: Some Monday Programming Humor For a Niche Audience (to help you jump-start the week)
item.description:
1. Guess we found Elon's alternative twitter account
2. She is back with more wisdom
3. As someone who used to bang the ENTER so hard, I approve of this
4. Yap, yap, yap
5
6. Some ancient wisdom for you all
7
8. How to give the entire IT department a heart attack
9
10. CTRL + C, CTRL + V
11
12
13. Project Managers be like
14
15. And finally I would like to share a young picture of @OP. Yap, this was me, just a rugged jacked nerd who loved video games. Now all grown up, I'm making games of my own, and my "A.I." algorithm has calculated that there is 95.413% chance that if you liked the humor of this dump, you will like the game too. So anyway, please wishlist, it helps small devs a lot : ) https://store.steampowered.com/app/2336120/Do_Not_Press_The_Button_To_Delete_The_Multiverse/ Happy week, ma dudes!

item.title: A super group I was not aware of
item.description: Oof Tatata is the name of Jason's band Jason Momoa plays bass and guitar, has acquired some dream instruments and even got to jam with some of his heroes. But he's never been in a band or played a gig before. That all changed last night with the debut performance by his new trio called Oof Tatata (Momoa applied to trademark the name on 9 April). We say new, but the three musicians involved go way back. "I've known Kenny Dale and Mike Hayes for about 20 years and these guys taught me how to play music," said the Game Of Thrones alumni introducing the trio's set at the New Zealand wrap party at the end of filming for the long-awaited Minecraft film. "But I never wanted to play in a band, but now times have changed so this is the first show." He picked a great occasion for the debut gig because fellow Minecraft castmates Danielle Brookes, and Jack Black (alongside Rita Ora) were on hand to help out – the latter two duetting on a celebratory take on Stevie Nicks' classic Edge OF Seventeen with Momoa on Fender Precision Bass, Hayes on Buddy Guy Strat and Dale on drums. https://www.musicradar.com/news/twenty-years-in-the-making-jason-momoa-performs-his-first-ever-live-show-as-a-bassist-covering-black-sabbath-hendrix-zeppelin-metallica-and-stevie-nicks-with-jack-black-danielle-brooks-and-rita-ora-joining-as-guests

item.title: You can’t take the sky from me
item.description: Took me a few months and several tries to 3D print, paint and add lights. Not 100% screen accurate but I’m happy with it. Front Page edit: Thank you all for the wonderful comments. I put a lot of work into this model and I'm glad you all seem to like it. For those of you who asked I got the STLs from here: https://www.gambody.com/3d-models/serenity-spaceship-firefly

item.title: The most important vote of your life so far...
item.description: Register to Vote Check your voter registry. Republicans havre been purging people from the voting roles Get up early on polling day, show up when the polls open, make sure you vote. Republicans are trying to stop vote counting and close polls early Democracy is under attack. you need to vote to save it.

item.title: Remember Team Fortress 2?
item.description: I don't care if this gets lost in usersub or gets downvoted to hell. if even one person sees this, it's a win for me. I'll try to keep this brief, but just like the image says, Team Fortress 2, one of the most popular PC games of all time, published by Valve, has been suffering for more than half a decade. Casual matchmaking has been overrun by bots that use aim hacks and spam filth, profanity, and ear rape over voice chat and in text chat. The hosters of these bots have actually doxxed some human players, and in one case even sent police to one if TF2's more popular content creators via a falsified bomb threat. 2 years ago, the TF2 community put together an event called SaveTF2, where we showed our ongoing love for a game that, then, was nearing 15 years old. The Valve team responded, but did next to nothing to fix the ongoing bot issue. Today, the gaming community is trying again. There is a petition up right now at save.tf that has just recently hit over 100k signatures. Our plan is to physically print the names of these petitioners and send the papers to Valve in the same way World of Warcraft players did when they organized a protest of their own regarding their ignored pleas for some a legacy update. Look, the point is, we need as many voices as we can to be heard by Valve, because even though Team Fortress 2 is now 17 years old, it still has a dedicated community. And this community is done seeing its beloved game in such an unplayable state, while Valve continues to add seasonal microtransactions and cosmetics to it. Upvote me, downvote me, ignore me completely. I really don't care. But I am sickened by how Valve has treated their most popular game, and today is the day Team Fortress 2's playerbase says, "enough is enough." Thanks for reading. tl;dr Team Fortress 2 is being ignored by its publisher, Valve, even after 5+ years of cheating, vulgar bots. Theres a petition and Twitter movement happening. join, or don't.
item.dc:creator: randomkeyboardsmash

item.title: Also, corn. Hours and hours of nothing but corn.

item.title: Just in time

item.title: Crank Cash Register Project - Restoration Complete!
item.description:
Three weeks and one sore back later, I've finished work on my 1911 National Cash Register! Most of you know the process by now, but for those who are new to these posts, I enjoy a hobby of tracking down old, worn down antique cash registers (and sometimes typewriters) to clean up, repair, and ultimately restore back to working condition within reasonable limits. This is the final update of an ongoing restoration project I started a few weeks ago. You can find the previous updates linked below. :) Part 1: https://imgur.com/gallery/3e4uTnd Part 2: https://imgur.com/gallery/56gCH4U Part 3: https://imgur.com/gallery/DWh24sx
So! Last I left off, I had some boxes yet to tick before this machine was done. Most pressing on that list was the condition of the register's wooden base. I had previously repaired a break along one of the joints where the wood had been pulled apart, and now I need to deal with the overall condition of the wood itself.
Dust and dirt aside, the wood isn't in awful condition for 123 years old. There's a lot of wear and tear, some chipping, and a ton of discoloration. What I'm mainly looking at by this stage is the remaining bits of original lacquer tucked away in the grooves, shown here as dark patches. By now the majority of that veneer is gone and what's left can't just be painted over and forgotten about. It has to come off, which means it's time to mask up, break out the picks and sanding paper, and get to work!
After a half hour or so of sanding, starting at 200 grit and working my way up to 1000, the original color of the wood is showing through beautifully. The old lacquer came off without any need for chemical stripping, just some squinting and gentle picking, and what remained came out with the sanding. I'm not 100% on the species of the wood, but I've heard everything from mahogany to cherry. It's a hardwood, I can say that much. The rest is a mystery to me.
Sorry for the extreme close-up. @.@ I chose to stain the wood with what Minwax fancily calls "Sedona Red," and what I call "Goes-Well-With-Brass-Cherry." I wanted something that matched up to the popular styles of wood at the time and, biased as I am, I went with the same shade of red that the old wood floor in my workroom has. Two coats applied over the course of two days. My garage smells very carpenter-y now.
I think it turned out pretty great compared to where it was before! The wood no longer snags at you when you touch it, it's smooth as glass baby! Now came the time to stop procrastinating and get to the hard stuff: FINAL REASSEMBLY. (Audience: ooooh, aaaah...)
First off, I took a minute to clean the nickel plated rings of each button. Rubbing alcohol and a scotch pad made short work of it. Someday later I might investigate how to clear up the faces of each button, but prior experience tells me I don't want to spend an entire weekend prying off those rings. They're stupidly easy to crack and I don't have a donor machine to borrow from... so they'll wait.
Next, I put on the brass backplates for each row of keys as well as the nickel shields that protect the counters. These counter windows ultimately get locked behind hinged covers which are only meant to be accessible to a shop owner. The register keeps a rolling tally of every transaction type that was recorded, as well as a running total in the row just above the keys.
Before I can do any more assembly on the register body, I need to rebuild the drawer box it sits on. This box was the key part of the machine that the previous owner had removed (who knows why) more than half of the fasteners which led to the wood base coming loose and partially breaking. I have to assume this happened when it was being transported or slid around, and it's insanely lucky that the box didn't simply fold into itself from the misalignment.
Once the center drawer rail is installed (upside-down in this picture, because I'm a dunce who makes mistakes too) the three outer walls are secured to the wood base with new fasteners. Rather than ditter around with seven new fasteners and three rusting ones, I opted to just replace all of them.
Next comes the lid, as well as the reinforcing iron bars that bridge the walls horizontally. These take the full weight of the machine and help distribute it across the rest of the box, and are easily the only reason why the register survived this long.
(I really should have trimmed these photos so you're not having to stare at my floor. Ack!) After the drawer box is screwed together, the register core gets mounted on top. My spine is still mildly furious with me for the awkward deadlift. Four chunky bolts get ratcheted in from inside the drawer at each corner, and they are all that hold the two halves together.
Next comes the fun part: the assembly of the outer chassis! Taking detailed photos during disassembly three weeks ago saved my bacon, since not every panel requires the same screws and there's really no visual clues to help you know which one goes where. Use the wrong screw on the wrong step and suddenly you're lacking that critical fastener later on, and you don't know where it went. Some people have nightmares about showing up to work naked or falling through infinity. I have nightmares about getting 95% through a restoration and realizing I have to reverse five hours of work to fix an error.
The right panel goes on easily, and it's worth pointing out the four holes lining the top of it. There is a piece of glass behind them, and behind that are four digits which indicate how many times the register has been cycled. These run on a standalone counter that cannot be reset manually without disassembling the machine. Anti-theft measures were pretty wild in the year before the Titanic sunk.
HAN-DUL. I didn't end up doing much more than some simple cleaning for the handle and the Bakelite knob. Some soap with degreaser took off all the old grime and since the metal is plated, I wasn't going to overpolish it and risk digging into the nickel. I rubbed a bit of machine lubricant along the cog and in the knob shaft, then flagellated myself for the filthy thoughts that followed.
Took a quick shot of one of the bell lock, which is mounted to the reverse side of the rearmost panel. Given this is the side that faces the customer, I thought it was interesting they would design a lock here.
Aw yeah, mechporn. I didn't end up having to do any hands-on cleaning of the internal parts beyond blowing out the dust and barbershop trimming with compressed air. These machines quite frankly don't seem to give a good god damn about their own age. What little rust there is inside of it stopped spreading well before I was born. NCR was not stingy on the grease.
And on goes the back panel! I really love how the wood compliments the cleaned brass. You can see a few spots near the top that look like droplet marks, and these are discolorations caused by the grime and who knows what else that collected on the metal. These won't come off under a buffing wheel - so far in my experience, light sanding is the only way to remove them and in doing so you just end up causing more of a mess. I opted to leave them be.
Apologies for any strained necks! I took a snap of the back of this plate to give you guys an idea of how the glass gets mounted into place. The frames are super lightweight and the screws are stupidly tiny. Plus they're brass heads that will self-destruct their screwdriver slots if you look at them funny. :')
That plate with all the glass windows extends from the top of the key rows to the... cap. Which I didn't take photos of during the rebuild. I'm just now realizing that. For the love of god. XD Alright, in lieu of photos, we have our *imagination!* That top cap is actually two separate pieces which, when screwed together, close around the glass to hold it in place. The glass itself is completely clear and the black background is actually a painted metal place, which has a duplicate facing out the other side for the customer. The whole thing seats onto the rectangular rim formed by the four side panels, and bolts directly into the register's frame.
The National Cash Register trademark plate screws right into the wood, and cleaning these is a royal pain... but worth it. It's hard to see, but the "National Cash Register, Daytoy Ohio" bit is stamped in relief.
All that's left to do after that is to mount the receipt printer cover, pop on the marble shelf, and slide in the cash drawer! Was I so exhausted after 11 straight hours of work yesterday that I forgot to take pictures of these too? Maybe! I ain't no snitch. But yes, yes, I was extremely tired. >
Here she is, finally finished! This is my first crank operated cash register and I can say without a doubt that the amount of hours I put into this single machine dwarfs the time I've put into all the others that came before. This was time consuming from start to finish, and absolutely worth it for the end result.
It goes without saying, I'm keeping this one. I knew that when I was driving home with it. It's a beauty, every bit of it works, and it really showcases the skills I've developed in taking on restorations like these. And, as always, I know you folks are really just here for one thing: The Clickity-Clack Ding.
Best I can do is tree fiddy. ;) Ain't goin' no lower, I know what I got.
Obligatory finish line horse. Artist: Rocket-Lawnchair
I thought I'd get a little showy and cobble together a before n' after photo in the trusty ol' MSPaint. I'm really proud of how well this came out. It's clean, repaired, and still has all the little bits of age it's earned over the last century and change. There's really no feeling like stepping back from a project that took so much time and patience, and just admiring what it is. It's good stuff. ^_^ I'm able to do these restorations largely in part thanks to the generosity of fellow users! If you feel like contributing a few dollars toward the next project, feel free to pop by my Kofi and say hello! https://ko-fi.com/elbowdeepinahorse/ Thank you all for following and upvoting this restoration! I'm going to go ice my shoulder now. :D
item.dc:creator: ElbowDeepInAHorse

greetings online friends
either way it looks human and i'm scared
mmm hmm
i'd "accidentally" spill liquids on them
always me
indeed
one stop shopping
it's also not a garden
too easy. got anymore brain busters, baldur?
sorry about this one
i'm on team meme
nah those are 100% cookies
thinkin about them jeans
clever
dang
we still had these random places in the 80s/90s
tough to get a wifi signal sometimes
that's my set.. have a great night gang!!!

item.title: Why you should not do something unless you do it correctly.
item.description:
"Yesterday during an ATLAS PRESERVATION presentation, crews were dispatched to Saint Nick’s Cemetery in Reserve Township after an approximately 1,400lb tombstone had fallen onto a young male’s leg leaving him trapped... Thank you to Reserve VFD - Station 317 and Reserve Township Police for all their help!"
The inspection of the stone prior to cleaning requires you to touch it. Push on it, shake it, and attempt to move it. Required. Because of this kind of thing. This is more common than you probably realize and this guy is lucky- it could have been his head.

item.title: Fuck the CCP
item.description: Posting this on behalf of the people of Hong Kong and China who would get locked up for doing as much. There are people in parts of the world like this who have lost their rights to freedom speech and protest because of dictators. We need a stronger Free World to help those in places like China and HK get their freedom back. Americans, don't allow your own dictator-wannabe to ever be elected again. It'll be the last election you get otherwise, and we could really do with a sane, non-Nazi USA at the Free World's table right now.
item.dc:creator: ChurBlackSheep

item.title: Hopefully this Ernest clip in Intergalactic Quality will cheer you up if you're feeling down. Enjoy!
item.description: Source: Ernest Goes to Jail (1990) #intergalacticqualitygifs (Check WiFi) Sorry for the repost, I am just going through some of my stuff that makes me happy until I can upload fully again. Hope you are all doing well :) Ernest post #2 today: https://imgur.com/gallery/adXDnUe Ernest post #3 today: https://imgur.com/gallery/fsa3tYb Ernest post #4 today: https://imgur.com/gallery/ZXTHDFg Ernest post #5 today: https://imgur.com/gallery/k04CrkY Ernest post #6 today: https://imgur.com/gallery/XUhLymK Ernest post #7 today: https://imgur.com/gallery/G8nvndY If you don't click on any of these please just check this one out XD: https://imgur.com/gallery/F3rewjA
